one_line_takeaway: AgentVisor mediates every LLM-agent tool call through a context-isolated, OS-hypervisor-inspired semantic auditor (Suitability/Taint/Integrity checks) with one-shot self-correction via structured exceptions, driving prompt-injection ASR to near-zero (0.65% average) while losing only 1.45% average utility versus no defense, outperforming 8 prior detection- and mitigation-based baselines on OpenPromptInjection and AgentDojo.
key_finding: On the GPT-4o backbone, AgentVisor reduces direct-injection ASR to 0.00% across all seven attack strategies (vs. No Defense ASR mostly >88%, e.g. 97.00% for Direct, 98.63% for SYSTEM) while keeping UA above 76% even under the hardest Combined attack (76.00% UA) and above 83% for most others; the strongest baseline on ASR, DataSentinel, achieves near-zero ASR but its utility collapses (UA about 0-3%). For indirect injection, AgentVisor keeps ASR below 1.7% for six of seven attacks and 0.69% for the hardest 'Important' attack (vs. No Defense's 45.14% ASR on Important), while preserving UA in the 63-78% range; DeBERTa's detection-based defense achieves low ASR but severe over-defense (UA around 12-25%). Averaged across all reported settings, AgentVisor's overall ASR figure quoted in the abstract/intro is 0.65%, with only 1.45% average utility loss relative to No Defense. The ablation shows each STI stage is specialized: removing Suitability spikes direct-injection ASR to 38.95%; removing Taint raises indirect ASR to 13.33%; removing Integrity raises indirect ASR to 8.89%. Removing self-correction (block-only) collapses UA to near zero (0.00% direct, 13.33% indirect), while one-shot Semantic Fault Recovery restores UA to 85.56% (direct) and 66.67% (indirect), showing self-correction is essential to utility, not just the audit. The trade-off analysis shows N=1 correction round is near-optimal: extending to N=2 only raises indirect UA by 1.29% while costing 1.45x latency, and N=3 costs 1.90x latency for negligible further gain. In the adaptive-attack setting targeting the defense layer itself, a 'Naive Visor' (without context isolation) collapses to 53.95% UA on direct injection, while AgentVisor keeps 0% ASR and 86.85% UA, attributed to architectural context isolation (the Visor never sees raw untrusted content). Latency overhead is about 1.4x in benign conditions and rises to 2.32x (direct) / 1.71x (indirect) under attack due to the extra self-correction pass. Results replicate on a GLM-4.7 backbone in the appendix with similar qualitative patterns (e.g., AgentVisor achieves 0.00% ASR across all direct-injection attacks and 0-1.55% ASR on indirect injection).
core_contribution: AgentVisor, a virtualization-inspired defense architecture for tool-using LLM agents that mediates every proposed tool call through a trusted 'Visor' component, analogous to an OS hypervisor mediating a guest OS's privileged operations. The Visor audits each tool-call proposal from the target agent (the 'Guest') using a three-stage STI protocol -- Suitability (is the tool permitted under the system role/policy), Taint (is the tool call's implied goal aligned with user intent and task state), and Integrity (are the tool arguments consistent with user-specified entities) -- and, on failure, synthesizes a structured 'Semantic Exception' (type, violated_rule, rationale, constraints, allowed_objective) that the Guest uses to regenerate a corrected tool call exactly once (one-shot self-correction) before execution. Critically, the Visor is architecturally blind to raw untrusted external context Ct; it only sees the trusted system instruction, the user query, and a sanitized/structured execution history with raw observations stripped out.
threat_model: An adversary who cannot modify the agent, its system instruction, or the tool implementations/execution environment, but can manipulate inputs delivered to the agent. Two attack classes are considered: (1) Direct prompt injection, where the adversary acts as the user and appends a malicious instruction directly to the query to override system constraints; direct injection is assumed not to rely on external context. (2) Indirect prompt injection, where the user query is benign but the adversary poisons externally retrieved content (webpages, emails, documents) with hidden or overt malicious instructions that the agent later processes during multi-step tool use, aiming to hijack a tool call at some later step.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Section 8): (1) Computational overhead -- the hypervisor layer adds inference latency and token cost, only partially offset by limiting self-correction to one shot. (2) Long-context scalability -- as interaction history grows, the Visor's performance may be constrained by the underlying LLM's context window, potentially degrading taint-analysis precision in very long conversations. (3) Multimodal generalization -- the current STI protocol is designed for textual prompt injection only; extending it to visual or audio-based injection in multimodal agents is left to future work. Additionally (not stated as a formal limitation but evident from the design): the Visor itself is an LLM-based semantic judge (e.g., Gemini-2.5-Flash) rather than a formally verified mechanism, so its Suitability/Taint/Integrity judgments are themselves susceptible to the general unreliability of LLM-based semantic classification, even though the paper argues architectural context isolation blunts adaptive attacks against it specifically.

datasets_used: OpenPromptInjection (Liu et al., 2024b) -- 7 NLP tasks combined pairwise as target/injection tasks, 100 sampled examples per task-pair combination, 4,900 total direct-injection attack cases

AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al., 2024) -- 4 interactive agent environments (Banking, Travel, Slack, Workplace) with 16, 20, 21, and 40 target tasks respectively, 629 total indirect-injection attack cases
quantitative_results: Overall (abstract): AgentVisor reduces ASR to 0.65% average with only 1.45% average utility decrease vs. No Defense
GPT-4o backbone, direct injection: AgentVisor ASR = 0.00% across all 7 attacks (Direct, Ignore, Escape, Fakecom, Combined, SYSTEM, Important); UA ranges 76.00% (Combined) to 90.00% (Fakecom)

GPT-4o backbone, indirect injection: AgentVisor ASR ranges 0.00% (Ignore/Escape*/Combined) to 1.69% (No Attack baseline case)/0.69% (Important, hardest attack), vs No Defense ASR of 45.14% on Important; UA ranges 63.75% (Important) to 78.25% (SYSTEM)
No Defense baseline (GPT-4o, direct injection) ASR mostly >88%, e.g. 97.00% (Direct), 98.63% (SYSTEM)
DataSentinel baseline (GPT-4o, direct injection): near-zero ASR but UA collapses to ~0-3%
DeBERTa baseline (indirect injection): UA drops to ~12-25% (severe over-defense)

Ablation (component removal, GPT-4o backbone): removing Suitability -> direct-injection ASR spikes to 38.95%; removing Taint -> indirect-injection ASR rises to 13.33%; removing Integrity -> indirect-injection ASR rises to 8.89%
Ablation (self-correction removal / block-only): UA collapses to 0.00% (direct) and 13.33% (indirect); full self-correction restores UA to 85.56% (direct) and 66.67% (indirect)

Correction-round trade-off: N=1 gives 91.72%/0.00% (UA/ASR direct) and 73.21%/1.73% (UA/ASR indirect) at 1.00x latency; N=2 gives 92.05%/0.00% and 74.50%/1.73% at 1.45x latency; N=3 gives 92.15%/0.00% and 74.85%/1.73% at 1.90x latency
Backbone-agnosticism: with Claude-Sonnet-4 as Visor, indirect-injection UA = 77.11% vs 64.27% for GLM-4.7 as Visor, while ASR stays near-zero (<4%) for all backbones

Adaptive attack targeting the defense layer directly: Naive Visor (no context isolation) UA collapses to 53.95% (direct injection) vs AgentVisor 86.85% UA with 0% ASR; indirect injection: Naive Visor 60.67% UA/4.91% ASR (roughly) vs No Defense 71.11% UA/6.53% ASR, per Fig. 7

Latency (Table 4): benign condition 2.12s (No Defense) vs 2.95s AgentVisor (1.39x) for direct injection, 6.45s vs 9.10s (1.41x) for indirect; attack condition 2.18s vs 5.05s (2.32x) direct, 6.58s vs 11.25s (1.71x) indirect
GLM-4.7 backbone (appendix) replicates pattern: direct-injection ASR = 0.00% across all 7 attacks for AgentVisor; indirect-injection ASR ranges 0.00%-1.55%
baselines_compared: DataSentinel (Liu et al., 2025) -- detection-based, evaluated only on direct injection (its original design scope)
MELON (Zhu et al., 2025) -- detection-based, evaluated only on indirect injection (its original design scope)
